sourceDefinitions.description: Root-sum-square over coherent axis-0 and axis-1 charged sums divided by sqrt(2), with neutral-axis root-sum-square.

selectionLawAudits.description: Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows with the closest unscaled source magnitudes.
Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows with the smallest branch spread, then closest unscaled source magnitudes.
Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows that maximize the weaker unscaled source magnitude.
Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows with the closest Phase302-scaled source magnitudes.
Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows with the smallest branch spread, then closest Phase302-scaled source magnitudes.
Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows that maximize the weaker Phase302-scaled source magnitude.
Select same-transition stable W/Z rows with the closest unscaled source magnitudes.
Select same-transition stable W/Z rows with the closest Phase302-scaled source magnitudes.

p302ScaledStableCommonSelectionLaw.description: Select the stable decoupled W/Z rows that maximize the weaker Phase302-scaled source magnitude.

nextRequiredArtifact: A new theorem-backed W source-definition family that does not require the Phase379-suppressed carrier axis, if that is physically intended.
A GU-native observed electroweak projection/namespace map separating diagnostic carrier axes from physical photon/W/Z axes.
A derivation explaining why the Phase307 canonical charged-ladder W row must use the Phase379-suppressed axis despite the response-image diagnostic.
Separate W and Z Phase201 source rows with source-lineage ids, raw/common/target gates, stability sidecars, derivation ids, and GeV normalization.
decision: No current Phase307 predeclared W/Z selector can avoid the Phase379-suppressed axis. All persisted Phase307 W source definitions use the canonical charged axes 0 and 1, so every predeclared selector's W row uses suppressed carrier axis 1. The Phase381 conflict therefore cannot be repaired inside the existing Phase307 selector space; progress requires a new source-definition theorem, an observed electroweak projection/namespace map, or a derivation that makes the suppressed axis physically required.
